does: This paper evaluates multiple machine unlearning algorithms applied to medical imaging models, assessing their ability to remove the influence of specific training samples while preserving model performance on retained data. The authors benchmark several unlearning techniques on medical imaging tasks, measuring effectiveness on both forget sets (samples to remove) and retain sets (samples to keep), while analyzing fairness with respect to gender and performance degradation. Key findings show that current unlearning methods successfully eliminate target samples without gender bias, but substantially degrade model generalization especially when forgetting larger data subsets, introduce bias toward easy or hard samples, and impose significant computational costs for hyperparameter tuning. The work systematically characterizes the trade-offs inherent in existing unlearning approaches within the medical imaging domain.
connects: This work builds on the emerging machine unlearning literature that addresses privacy and the legal right to be forgotten, extending these concepts specifically to medical imaging where patient privacy and data rights are particularly sensitive. It engages with prior research on unlearning algorithms developed in general machine learning contexts, adapting and evaluating them in healthcare settings. The paper connects to broader work on model editing, privacy-preserving machine learning, and fairness in medical AI systems. It extends prior unlearning studies by conducting systematic empirical evaluation in a specialized domain and introducing new evaluation dimensions including gender fairness and sample-difficulty bias, which had not been thoroughly analyzed in earlier unlearning work.
enables: This work identifies concrete limitations of current unlearning methods that must be addressed before deployment in clinical settings, opening pathways for developing improved algorithms that better preserve generalization while removing specific samples. It motivates research into unlearning techniques specifically designed for medical imaging that account for the unique challenges identified: computational efficiency for clinical deployment, maintaining performance on retention sets, and avoiding bias against sample subgroups. The findings suggest opportunities for algorithmic innovations combining selective retraining, knowledge distillation, or regularization approaches tailored to medical data. Furthermore, it establishes unlearning as an important component of responsible AI in healthcare, enabling future work on practical frameworks for patient data rights and regulatory compliance in medical imaging systems.
